title: Roast Chicken with Fresh Thyme
intro: A gloriously simple roast that relies on high heat and a bone-dry skin to deliver shattering crackle over juicy meat. Nothing but salt, pepper, and heat between you and perfection.
steps: Pat the chicken completely dry inside and out, then let it sit uncovered in the fridge for an hour if time allows.
Heat the oven to 450F and season the cavity and skin generously with salt and pepper.
Truss the legs together with kitchen string and tuck the wing tips underneath the body.
Set the bird breast-up in a roasting pan or ovenproof skillet and slide it into the center of the oven.
Roast undisturbed for about 50 to 60 minutes until the skin is deep golden and the thigh juices run clear.
Rest the chicken for 15 minutes, then brush the skin with softened butter whisked with mustard and thyme leaves.
Carve and serve, spooning the pan juices over each portion.
tip: Do not baste or open the oven; a stable high heat gives you the crispest skin.

title: Classic Coq au Vin
intro: Chicken braised slowly in red wine with smoky bacon, mushrooms, and pearl onions until the sauce turns velvety and deep. This is French country cooking at its most soul-warming.
steps: Brown the bacon in a Dutch oven, then remove it and sear the seasoned chicken in the fat until golden on all sides.
Set the chicken aside and soften the pearl onions and garlic in the same pot.
Sprinkle in the flour and stir for a minute to cook out the raw taste.
Pour in the wine and stock, scraping up the browned bits, then return the chicken and bacon with the thyme and bay.
Cover and simmer gently for about 75 minutes until the chicken is fork-tender.
Saute the mushrooms in butter separately until golden and stir them into the braise.
Simmer uncovered to thicken the sauce, then discard the herbs and serve hot.
tip: Choose a wine you would happily drink; a harsh bottle makes a harsh sauce.

title: Engagement Roast Chicken
intro: A lemon-and-herb roast so reliably delicious it has a reputation for winning hearts. The bird perfumes with garlic and citrus while the pan juices become a bright, effortless sauce.
steps: Heat the oven to 425F and pat the chicken dry, seasoning the cavity well with salt and pepper.
Stuff the cavity with the thyme, both lemon halves, and the halved head of garlic.
Brush the skin all over with melted butter and season generously.
Scatter the sliced onion across a roasting pan and set the chicken on top.
Roast for about 70 minutes until the juices run clear and the skin is bronzed.
Rest the chicken loosely tented with foil for 15 minutes.
Deglaze the onions and pan drippings with stock over the stove, then strain into a sauce to spoon over the carved bird.
tip: Squeeze the roasted lemon from the cavity into the pan sauce for a bright lift.

title: Crispy Chicken with Garlic Butter
intro: Pan-seared chicken breasts finished with a foaming garlic-thyme butter that bastes the meat to a glossy shine. Fast, restaurant-sharp, and endlessly repeatable on a weeknight.
steps: Season the chicken generously and heat the oil in a heavy skillet until shimmering.
Lay the breasts skin-side down and press gently, cooking undisturbed until deeply golden.
Flip the chicken and add the butter, smashed garlic, and thyme to the pan.
Tilt the skillet and spoon the foaming butter continuously over the meat for two minutes.
Cook until the internal temperature reaches 165F, then squeeze in the lemon.
Rest the chicken for five minutes before slicing.
Serve drizzled with the garlic butter from the pan.
tip: Start the chicken in a not-too-hot pan so the skin renders slowly and crisps evenly.

title: Chicken Tikka Masala
intro: Yogurt-marinated chicken charred and simmered in a creamy, spiced tomato sauce that clings to every piece. Fragrant with garam masala and just enough chili to warm the back of the throat.
steps: Marinate the chicken in yogurt, half the ginger-garlic paste, and half the spices for at least an hour.
Sear the marinated chicken in a hot pan until charred at the edges, then set aside.
Soften the onion in ghee, then stir in the remaining ginger-garlic paste and spices until fragrant.
Pour in the crushed tomatoes and simmer until the sauce darkens and thickens.
Blend the sauce smooth if you like, then return it to the pan and stir in the cream.
Add the seared chicken and simmer gently until cooked through and coated.
Finish with a pinch of garam masala and serve with rice or naan.
tip: A quick blitz under the broiler on the marinated chicken mimics tandoor char at home.

title: Wiener Schnitzel-Style Chicken
intro: Thin chicken cutlets pounded delicate, breaded, and fried until impossibly crisp and golden. A squeeze of lemon is all this Austrian-inspired classic needs.
steps: Pound each butterflied breast between plastic wrap until thin and even.
Season the flour with salt, pepper, and paprika, then set up flour, egg, and breadcrumb stations.
Dredge each cutlet in flour, dip in egg, then coat fully in breadcrumbs.
Heat the oil in a wide skillet until a crumb sizzles on contact.
Fry the cutlets one or two at a time until golden brown on each side.
Drain on paper towels and season lightly with salt.
Serve at once with lemon wedges for squeezing.
tip: Swirl the pan as the cutlet fries so hot oil washes over the top and puffs the crust.

title: Chicken Cacciatore
intro: A rustic hunter's braise of chicken with peppers, tomatoes, and herbs that tastes like a slow Sunday in the Italian countryside. The sauce reduces into something rich enough to mop with crusty bread.
ingredients: 1 can (28 oz) whole tomatoes, crushed by hand
steps: Season the chicken and brown it well in olive oil, then remove to a plate.
Soften the onion, pepper, and mushrooms in the same pan until they release their liquid.
Add the garlic and cook until fragrant, then pour in the wine to deglaze.
Stir in the crushed tomatoes and rosemary and bring to a simmer.
Nestle the chicken back into the sauce and cover partially.
Braise gently for about 40 minutes until the chicken is tender and the sauce thickens.
Adjust seasoning and serve over polenta or with warm bread.
tip: Crushing whole tomatoes by hand gives the sauce a livelier texture than pureed ones.

title: Chicken Suprême with Pan Sauce
intro: A skin-on chicken breast cooked to a burnished crisp, then paired with a glossy, mustard-tinged pan sauce. Elegant French technique that fits a single skillet.
steps: Season the breasts and place skin-side down in a cold skillet with a little butter.
Cook over medium heat so the fat renders and the skin turns crisp and golden.
Flip and finish cooking until the meat reaches 160F, then rest on a plate.
Soften the shallot in the pan drippings, then pour in the wine and reduce by half.
Add the stock and simmer until slightly thickened.
Whisk in the mustard, cream, and remaining butter off the heat, then fold in the tarragon.
Slice the chicken and spoon the sauce generously over the top.
tip: Starting the skin in a cold pan renders the fat gently for a shatteringly crisp finish.

title: Berbere Spiced Chicken Stew
intro: An Ethiopian-rooted doro wat with chicken simmered in a deep, spice-layered onion sauce built on fiery berbere. Slow-cooked onions give it a jammy sweetness that balances the heat.
steps: Cook the onions in a dry pot over low heat, stirring often, until soft and deeply reduced.
Stir in the niter kibbeh and ginger-garlic paste and cook until fragrant.
Add the berbere and tomato paste and toast briefly to bloom the spices.
Pour in the stock and bring to a gentle simmer.
Nestle in the drumsticks, cover, and braise until the meat is falling-tender.
Score the hard-boiled eggs and add them to warm through in the sauce.
Season with salt and serve with injera or rice.
tip: Take your time softening the onions; that slow foundation defines the whole stew.

title: Za'atar Roast Chicken with Sumac
intro: Chicken thighs roasted with red onion, za'atar, and tangy sumac until the skin crisps and the pan fills with bright, herby juices. A vibrant sheet-pan dinner bursting with Middle Eastern flavor.
steps: Whisk the olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, za'atar, and half the sumac into a marinade.
Toss the chicken and onion wedges in the marinade and let sit for 30 minutes.
Arrange everything skin-side up on a sheet pan and season with salt and pepper.
Roast at 425F until the skin is crisp and the onions are charred at the edges.
Check that the thighs reach 175F for tender, pull-apart meat.
Scatter the remaining sumac and fresh parsley over the top.
Serve with the pan juices and warm flatbread.
tip: Sprinkle the second hit of sumac after roasting to keep its lemony tang bright.

title: Chicken in Milk
intro: A whole chicken pot-roasted in milk with lemon zest, garlic, and sage until the liquid breaks into golden, savory curds. Strange to read, unforgettable to eat.
ingredients: Zest of 2 lemons, peeled in strips
steps: Season the chicken and brown it all over in butter and oil in a snug Dutch oven.
Remove the chicken and pour off most of the excess fat.
Return the chicken to the pot and add the milk, cinnamon, lemon zest, garlic, and sage.
Bring to a bare simmer, then cover partially and transfer to a 375F oven.
Cook for about 90 minutes, basting once or twice, until the chicken is tender and the sauce curdles into golden nuggets.
Rest the chicken briefly, then break it into portions.
Spoon the curds, garlic, and lemony sauce over each serving.
tip: Do not worry when the milk splits; those savory curds are the whole point of the dish.

title: Spatchcock Harissa Chicken
intro: A flattened chicken slathered in smoky harissa and honey, roasted fast and hot so every inch turns lacquered and crisp. Bold, sticky, and made for tearing apart at the table.
steps: Whisk the harissa, honey, oil, lemon juice, garlic, and cumin into a thick glaze.
Pat the spatchcocked chicken dry and rub the glaze over every surface.
Let it marinate for at least 30 minutes, or overnight for deeper flavor.
Lay the chicken skin-side up and flat on a lined sheet pan.
Roast at 425F until the skin is deeply caramelized and lacquered.
Confirm the thickest part of the thigh reaches 175F.
Rest for 10 minutes, then cut into pieces and serve with the pan juices.
tip: Spatchcocking flattens the bird so it cooks evenly and much faster than a whole roast.

title: Buttermilk Fried Chicken
intro: Chicken brined and soaked in herby buttermilk, then fried in a craggy, well-seasoned crust that stays crackling long after it leaves the oil. This is the gold standard of fried chicken.
steps: Toss the chicken with two tablespoons of salt and refrigerate for an hour to brine.
Submerge the chicken in buttermilk and let it soak for at least 30 minutes.
Whisk the flour with the remaining salt, garlic powder, onion powder, paprika, and cayenne.
Lift each piece from the buttermilk and dredge heavily in the seasoned flour, pressing to build craggy crust.
Heat the oil to 320F and fry the dark meat first, then the white, adjusting heat to hold temperature.
Fry each piece until deep golden and cooked to 165F inside.
Drain on a rack and rest a few minutes before serving.
tip: Fry at a moderate temperature so the thick crust cooks through without burning.

title: Chicken Marbella
intro: A sweet-and-savory dinner-party icon of chicken baked with prunes, olives, capers, and brown sugar until glazed and jammy. It tastes even better made a day ahead.
steps: Combine the chicken with garlic, oregano, prunes, olives, capers, vinegar, and oil in a bowl.
Cover and marinate in the fridge overnight for the deepest flavor.
Arrange the chicken and marinade in a single layer in a baking dish.
Sprinkle the brown sugar over the chicken and pour the white wine around it.
Bake at 375F, basting occasionally, until the chicken is glazed and cooked through.
Let it rest a few minutes so the juices settle.
Serve with the prunes, olives, and pan sauce spooned over the top.
tip: Marinating overnight is what makes this dish sing, so plan a day ahead.

title: Butter Chicken
intro: Tender chicken bathed in a silky tomato sauce mellowed with butter, cream, and warm spices. Rich and gently sweet, it is comfort food that feels like a celebration.
steps: Marinate the chicken in yogurt, half the ginger-garlic paste, and the chili powder for an hour.
Sear the chicken in a hot pan until lightly charred, then set aside.
Melt the butter and cook the remaining ginger-garlic paste with the cumin and garam masala.
Pour in the tomato puree and simmer until it thickens and deepens in color.
Stir in the cream and crushed fenugreek leaves for a smooth, glossy sauce.
Return the chicken and simmer gently until cooked through and tender.
Finish with a knob of butter and serve with basmati rice or naan.
tip: Rubbing dried fenugreek leaves between your palms before adding releases their aroma.
